items.title: Is the Age of Reading Over?
items.description: Subscribe here: Apple Podcasts | Spotify | YouTube | Overcast | Pocket Casts Over the past 18 months, scholars have debated—and likely will continue to long into the future—why voters returned Donald Trump to the presidency. One intriguing theory: It’s because Americans don’t read books anymore. Fewer than half of all adults reported reading a book of any kind in 2022; only 38 percent read a novel or short story. And the proportion of Americans reading for pleasure on any given day is plummeting, according to the latest Atlantic cover story, “ The End of Reading Is Here ,” by Rose Horowitch. We are all awash in text, of course: messages from friends, headlines, captions on videos. But not the kinds of rich, layered sentences that have shaped our brains for centuries and given rise to many academic disciplines. Hence, we are a populace susceptible to a man who can capture attention, stir emotion, and breezily contradict himself, because his words are not etched in stone, or anywhere, really. Horowitch joins Radio Atlantic to discuss what it means to live in a postliterate society, what we’ve lost, and how we might recover. The following is a transcript of the episode: Hanna Rosin: We’re going to have a conversation about the end of reading today. This idea, proposed in the most recent Atlantic cover story, is that the age of reading is turning out to be a short anomaly in human history and that we are fast entering a postliterate society. But before we do that, I want you to think about the arc of your own relationship with books. How did you read as a kid? In school? When did that change? Did you notice it change? What’s the last book you read, absorbed, and enjoyed? [ Music ] Rosin: I’m Hanna Rosin. This is Radio Atlantic . I’m guessing that you’ve heard someone lament about how students—in middle school, high school, or even in college—don’t read whole books anymore. Maybe you have a teacher friend who complains about having to assign excerpts or increasingly simple texts. Maybe you are a young student, and books seem annoying and time-consuming to you. Or maybe you’re an adult and just find yourself trying to read but instead reaching for your phone every couple of pages. The argument we’re discussing today is much broader than mere phone distraction and spans back to ancient Egypt. It’s about how reading shaped the human mind and then human society, and how that’s now veering in a different direction. Rosin: Do you think more people actually read your full article or heard about the debate on TikTok? Rose Horowitch: I hope that people read it, but I don’t know. Rosin: I’m talking today to the author of that cover story, Rose Horowitch, who is not the elderly professor type that you might normally assume wrote an Atlantic story called “ The End of Reading Is Here .” Horowitch: It’s so funny because I initially had a joke in there, like, You probably think I’m retirement age, reading this whole piece . Rosin: Rose is actually in her 20s. Horowitch: Yes. Rosin: She did grow up learning to love to read. But then as she got older, she found it harder. Horowitch: That’s a story probably a lot of people can relate to, of reading much more than when you were young and then gradually noticing it disappear. I think I was noticing more so when I would try to read a book that I would just, after every couple pages, want to check my phone in case I missed a notification. Or I would take a look at my watch to make sure that I didn’t have any notifications. So I had to start putting my phone in drawers or putting it in another room so that I really could not check it. So that was more what I noticed, yeah. Rosin: Right. So that’s your experience, and from your article, I’m seeing that’s the experience of lots of people. So just lay out the basics. In what ways and among whom has reading books declined? Horowitch: So reading books has declined very broadly, even among the groups of people that, you know, have been the most stalwart readers: Women and retirees and college graduates, they’re seeing a decline. We know that in the past two decades, the share of Americans that read a book or an article on any given day has declined by 40 percent. And that, as of 2022, which is the last year that we have really great data for, fewer than half of all Americans read a book at all in the year, and only 38 percent read a novel or short story. So we know that reading is just really in decline kind of across demographic groups. Rosin: And is it the types of books people now read, has that changed as well? I’m feeling very superior now ’cause I just finished my book last night, but anyway, keep going. ( Laughs .) Horowitch: ( Laughs .) Rosin: Yeah. Horowitch: So the types of books—we know that when people do read, the books that they’re reading are shorter and simpler than they used to be. New York Times best sellers have sentences that are about one-third shorter than they were a century ago. And I spoke with the head of the New York Public Library, and he was saying that young-adult fiction has really become one of their most popular offerings, even among people who are not young adults. So we’re definitely seeing that when people do pick up a book, the book that they read is shorter and the sentences are simpler and the vocabulary’s simpler, and that’s a real change as well. Rosin: You’ve written a lot about the decline of reading. I feel like it’s your subbeat now. ( Laughs .) Horowitch: Yes. Rosin: When you were doing research for this particular story, was there statistics or a comment from a teacher or something that really stuck with you or surprised you? Horowitch: I think some of the things that have been most chilling for me in reporting this out were the things that were really about children reading. And so we know that only 2 percent of American adults read to a child on any given day. And we do see that kind of every year older a child gets, the less they like to read. I spoke with one researcher who did focus groups, and high-school students described reading for pleasure as an alien practice. And so that really stuck out in my mind, just like, Okay, how did we get to this point, when kids view reading this way? Rosin: The way this argument is framed—and you just used the word chilling —is: There’s a problem. Who should we blame for the problem? Or a lot of people will read the article that way, and some will immediately go to Kids today, they’re always on their phones, and this generation of students . Some will say schools just gave up, and it’s this iteration of education that’s the problem. Do you, in your head, have a clear answer for who to blame? Horowitch: Well, it’s difficult to know exactly how much each factor plays into it. I definitely don’t think that it’s just kids these days . Rosin: That’s why I’m glad you wrote this story— Horowitch: Right. Rosin: —and not the retiring professor. Right. Horowitch: Yes. As a member of Gen Z, I’m like, If it’s kids these days, it’s me . But I think this is a story of technologies crowding out the time that people would’ve once spent reading books. We know that when the television was first invented, American households were spending tons of time watching that. Then obviously streaming, internet, smartphone, social media—it all just takes up so much of our time now that might have previously been devoted to reading a book. Rosin: We’re in some ways inundated with text. I feel on a daily basis I read a much higher quantity of actual words than I used to. So what is the difference? Horowitch: Yes, exactly. So people often talk about this as a literacy crisis, and I don’t think that that’s right at all. Our literacy rate is historically very high. We are probably reading more words than ever with social media, the number of emails you get for your job, and text messages with friends and everything. And so the difference is that we’re almost swimming in so much text that we just don’t actually have the time or, sort of, attention to kind of focus for an extended period on a rich or complex work, because our attention is turning to so many different things. And so that’s why I called it really a postliterate age instead of an illiterate age. Rosin: Explain postliterate . That’s a really important word in your argument. What does postliterate mean? Horowitch: Right. So what I was trying to get across is that reading is not just one mode of communication or one skill among many. When reading and writing first appeared about 6,000 years ago, it transformed society, and it changed the way people thought and politics and culture. And so I was trying to get across that I think that we’re living through a civilizational shift on that scale, where we’re gonna see changes in our consciousness, changes in the candidates we elect, and what our culture values, and even how we tell the story of our civilization. So that was what I was trying to get across by postliterate , that we can still read, but it’s an age where text is just not the kind of medium that we use to spread information or knowledge or culture or entertainment, and that that will have really far-reaching effects. Rosin: That was an excellent and fast summary, but I also feel like one thing about this argument is: It can seem like an elite argument that you and I and the people who read The Atlantic care about. It’s sort of ironic that you write—I don’t know how many words it was—a 7,000-word, very-well-written piece to explain how your generation doesn’t really read or have an attention span anymore. So I actually wanna dig in a little more slowly and deeply to why reading is important, ’cause we can summarize it quickly and just say to people reading is important. But I think until you actually understand the shifts that happened in human evolution or our brain via reading, it doesn’t seem that important. I can be like, I read what I want. Leave me alone . So maybe we’ll start with the work of Walter Ong. Walter Ong: Now, I understand that my task is to try to talk to you. Would you mind if I took my jacket off? It’s a little bit warmer in here— Rosin: I love his work because he’s a priest, so everything he writes about and thinks about has a spiritual dimension. He was also a teacher. So talk about his work around oral traditions versus written traditions and how they change the brain. Horowitch: Right. So I think the one thing that Ong really understood was that writing text is just a very different process than speaking it. Ong: Writing has reinforced the old. It’s made talkers talk more, but it’s made them talk differently. [ Music ] Horowitch: We tend to use more complex sentence structures and vocabulary. When you write something down, it gives you distance because you can see it reflected back at you. So you can go back to it and get new understanding. You can kind of take on a more complex analytical task. It’s a more dispassionate spread of information because you don’t immediately see the person who wrote it at the same time. Ong: It was the first time it ever occurred to him that every one of those definitions in the dictionary was something that somebody said this word means. The word didn’t come equipped with it. Somebody had to figure that out and say, This is what it means . Horowitch: And so he was arguing that it’s just very different and that this ushers in a sort of entirely new mode of thinking, a kind of rational, linear, analytical way of thinking. And that that is a precondition for a lot of the modern types of study that we think about today. Philosophy and modern science and history as an academic discipline, that those things required reading and writing. Ong: They can learn facts, but they cannot learn how to think in the way in which you have to think. The thought patterns that you have to be able to use cannot be mastered without writing. Rosin: Yeah, there’s this quote you reference from Amusing Ourselves to Death , the 1985 Neil Postman book: “Writing freezes speech.” And I think what he meant by that is that speech happens fluidly, but writing inserts a pause; and that pause allows us to reflect, shape our thoughts; and all this metacognition is happening when you write that doesn’t necessarily happen in speech, because speech is constantly moving. Is it something like that? Because I never actually thought in detail about what reading does to your brain versus writing. Horowitch: Yeah, so I think there’s two elements to it. One is that we just speak a lot faster than we write. And so it forces you to slow down and think a little bit more about what you’re putting out there. And then at the same time, when it is frozen before you, you can see it reflected back at you, this message, and so you can return to it for new understanding, think about how to reorder it, edit it, all these things that we can’t really do with speech, ’cause it just disappears into the ether. And so it sort of allows for this kind of logical way of thinking, and that, yeah, that quote from Postman really stuck out to me too. Rosin: I’m just realizing that podcasting, therefore, is for Neanderthals because— ( Laughs .) Horowitch: No. No, because it’s secondary orality. It’s mediated through this kind of writing, ’cause we’re talking about writing. Rosin: Okay. Secondary orality. I’ll take it. Okay, so that’s the long period that we’ve been through now, where we’ve evolved our brains, evolved a lot of human intellectual disciplines via reading, and now we’re entering towards postliteracy. What are the characteristics of a postliterate society, as opposed to the literate society you just explained? Horowitch: Yeah, so as part of my reporting for this piece, I interviewed a lot of cognitive scientists and was kind of trying to understand, Okay, what does happen to our consciousness? And I think what we see is that we know from neuroscience that what you practice is what your brain gets good at. And so as we’re reading less, we’re going to have less background knowledge to aid in comprehension. We’re going to have a less rational, analytical mode of thinking. We’re more accustomed to switching our attention and less accustomed to a kind of linear, extended focus on something. Even two decades ago, the average attention span on a screen was two-and-a-half minutes. And five years ago, it was 47 seconds. Rosin: There are some times when you describe it dispassionately, it almost sounds like a superpower, you know? Because speed is often thought of as good. We test students on how quickly they can do things. Tests are always timed. So I don’t know, just as you were talking, I was thinking, Could speed be some kind of intellectual superpower that we just don’t understand yet? Horowitch: Right. Well, I was trying to leave open that possibility. We do see, even as we see on tests, that it appears that IQs have been declining for the past two decades. At the same time, we are doing better on some forms of spatial rotation. So I definitely wanted to leave open the possibility that there could be these cognitive benefits that we just don’t understand and that this is a trade-off, but what we get will end up being worth it. And I think it’s too soon to tell if that’s possible. [ Music ] Rosin: After the break, who might thrive in a postliterate society? A clue: He’s currently president. [ Break ] Rosin: Okay, so that’s on the level of, like, an individual brain. There’s also an entire civilization because you start— Horowitch: ( Laughs .) Rosin: ( Laughs .) —the story at a broad civilization level. We can start at the easiest place, is with Trump. You describe him as the ideal president for a postliterate society. What does that mean? Horowitch: Yeah, so what I meant by that was that it’s sort of difficult to imagine Trump being elected in a country where information is primarily spread through text. But he really understands how to kind of grab attention and keep it in this postliterate age. So we see that he actually has kind of retrieved a lot of conventions from preliterate times. He contradicts himself as though there’s no record of what he said before. [ Music ] President Trump (in May): You’ll never have a leak. It’s very strong. If you had a knife, you can’t even cut it, it’s so strong, so powerful. It’s like powerful rubber. Reporter (in June): National Guard and police have been all over the Mall. How would these vandals have gotten so close to do something like that? Trump (in June): We didn’t have a lot of them then. Who would think that somebody would go into a pool and take a knife and start cutting it? Horowitch: He speaks with a lot of emotion, you know, often anger, and that’s just easier to remember than when someone gives a sort of dispassionate speech. Trump: I think it’s a disgrace. And I say that, and I say that, and that’s something that Nazi Germany would have done and did do. I think it’s a disgrace. Horowitch: We also see that he uses these kind of epithets that almost call back to Homer in the way that the poet described people. He says, “Sleepy Joe,” “Crooked Hillary,” and that’s just way easier to remember and repeat. And you just start to associate it. Trump: I call him Crooked Joe and Sleepy Joe. I find them to be equal actually. I do polls. Which is better: Crooked Joe ? Or Sleepy Joe ? And they poll about even. Horowitch: So we see that Trump has kind of understood how to entertain people, go viral, present things as very simple and authentic, and that’s helped him get elected. Rosin: The piece you wrote caused quite a stir, and especially among people who love books. People who love books tend to read The Atlantic , so maybe we’re getting a skewed sample. But let’s just say it caused quite a stir in the book-reading public, especially the publishing world. Is there any pushback that you’ve received that you think is interesting? Horowitch: Well, I think it’s interesting—there are a lot of positive signs for reading, which I did try to indicate in the story. Print-book sales were higher last year than they were a decade ago, and so that is certainly a fair critique. But at the same time, I tried to indicate in the piece, when I noted that, that what we’re seeing is that the people who are devoted to text are perhaps even more devoted to it than ever before. They spend more time reading than they used to, but they’re just becoming a smaller and smaller minority in society. Rosin: One thing I was thinking about is the democratization of text. When you wrote about people reading shorter sentences or YA novels, maybe one way of looking at this is that there used to be gatekeepers, people who would tell us what was literary, what was considered good to read, whereas now maybe you read what you want, which might be Colleen Hoover, which might be fan fiction, which might be someone’s online manifesto. And is that necessarily bad in the same way everything’s been democratized? Because if more people are buying books, it just means maybe they’re buying not literary books, but books. Horowitch: No, and that—I think that is a fair critique. And it sort of fits in with audiobooks almost, that they make it more accessible to read. And so I definitely do think it is a good thing if stories and information has been democratized and are accessible to more people. I think the challenge is just: Even as these things have become more widely available, we are still seeing a huge decline in reading. So it’s like, have people taken advantage of them? It’s not clear to me. Rosin: Yeah. And is your worry that because you write so much about reading inside the educational system, that down the road people will even stop reading those kinds of books because reading is just so much less fluidly integrated into education at all levels? Horowitch: Definitely we see in schools that teachers are just assigning far fewer books. I think a survey from last year found that most middle- and high-school teachers assign between zero and four books a year. And so we do see a big change where there’s a lot more emphasis on excerpts and on reading-comprehension drills. I guess my concern would be: You want everyone to have the option to read any work that they would want to, and so just making sure that students are equipped with the skills and mode of attention and practice that they can read whatever they want to instead of having some of it be unavailable to them. Rosin: Right. And I suppose there’s also a return to the analog, like reading retreats and that kind of thing. So there’s some kind of self-conscious niche, like I’m a reader . So it’s almost like your identity is you are a reader, and you go out to a reading club, and that’s who you are. It’s not everybody. It’s not a sign of a literate society. But it is your people. Horowitch: Yeah. One thing that really struck me in that Postman book was that before radio and television was invented, there was no way to be a reader, because there was no way to not be a reader. If you knew how to read, you had to. That was the only way to get information. And so it’s only now that being a reader is optional, that it really can be an identity marker. And we are seeing with some young people that there really is this drive towards more analog technologies. It seems to be a backlash to living in a digital age where everything feels ephemeral. I spoke with the head of the Cleveland Public Library, and he was saying that the young people that come in, they want a print book because they want something that feels tangible and concrete and that they can hold. So there certainly is that countervailing sign. Rosin: I know. It’s so funny. It’s like a print book is like a vinyl record. Horowitch: ( Laughs .) Rosin: ( Laughs .) Like, there will be stores that are like, We sell books and vinyl records . The bombshell of AI: One thing I noticed is that AI has an oddly formal way of speaking to you. Like, it’s a lot more like the letters you quoted from the average Civil War soldier than it is like texting. And so I know that, superficially, it seems like, Of course AI will destroy deep thinking and reading , but I also sense that AI returns a level of kind of full-sentences formality. Like, Here is how you should think through things . There is a kind of a return to discipline and what it’s presenting to you in a way that I find interesting. Horowitch: No, it’s funny. It’s sort of the ultimate irony that we talk about this technology that’s just gonna destroy thinking or whatever, and it’s text or, at least, now it communicates with us via text. And so the point that I tried to make is that we are actually living in a time where there’s more text. AI has led to a threefold increase in the number of books released on Amazon, more scientific papers being published. And a lot of this text at first brush appears polished and professional and something that you might trust and take in. And so that’s why I think it’s more important than ever to cultivate your powers of critical thinking and discernment, because AI is creating this superabundance of text, and we need to be able to sift through it. Rosin: Maybe that’s a useful way to think about this argument, that it’s not just about books. It’s not just about novels. It is about taking note of a world in which, because you are inundated with text, you can have this feeling that you are, in fact, reading a lot, but maybe pay attention to the ways in which whatever reading you’re doing is eroding an ability to think critically, think deeply, think independently. Just note that evolution or change. [ Music ] Horowitch: No, and I think that that has been true for so long. But one thing that I think we might be seeing now is that for a while at least, a lot of information on social media was presented in text. And now we sort of see with new capabilities just so much more short-form video. TikToker 1: The United States of America is becoming postliterate. TikToker 2: The end of reading is here, so I wanna talk about why this is a reality that I refuse to accept. Over the weekend, I read The Atlantic ’s August cover story, about the death of reading. TikToker 3: According to the National Endowment for the Arts, fewer than half of American adults reported having read any book in 2022. Horowitch: So I do wonder if we will get to a point where we’re not even reading more text, because it’s just videos now. TikToker 4: I’m about to talk about the death of reading on the app that is allegedly killing it. Stay with me. Rosin: Yeah, there was a really interesting interview with one of the leading figures at The New York Times who talked about the Times shifting very explicitly towards video, and that seemed like a moment. Partly just because we’re journalists, but we do think of The New York Times as a bastion of sort of literary, straight newsgathering. So it’s something interesting. And them acknowledging the future is video, we haven’t really even talked about that. We’ve been pretending as if we’re going to continue to live in a world inundated with text, but that may not be true at all. I know you interviewed a lot of professors, researchers. Did they have ideas beyond, Woe is me. I wish more people would read books ? Do they have method strategies, anything they employ that wasn’t just kind of, Do like we used to do ? Horowitch: Well, I think there are some signs that there’s a little bit of a backlash brewing. Certainly, we see it against tech in schools, you know, states banning cellphones during the school day. And when Texas did so, a district in Dallas saw 200,000 more library books checked out. So I think that— Rosin: Wait—explain the experiment again. What exactly happened? Horowitch: So Texas banned cellphones during the school day, and nearly two dozen states did it. And then the year after that happened, there were 200,000 more library books checked out at this Dallas school district than there had been the year before. So it did seem that I guess when people had more time and there was nothing else they could do, they were like, Okay, I’ll read a book . Rosin: That actually seems very simple and significant ’cause so many schools are banning cellphones during the day that it’s just out of sheer boredom. You’re like, Okay, fine, I’ll read a book. My teacher says it’s interesting. I’ll try it out. Horowitch: Right. Exactly. And so we don’t know that it inherently leads to better grades or people doing better in schools. It seems like the evidence is mixed there. But it does seem like it definitely forces people to just, Okay, we have so much more time back, so either we’re gonna hang out with each other, we’re gonna get a book, maybe do all of the above . So that’s a change. But in terms of things that we can do to kind of address this, I guess the challenge is, because I think it is such a big shift, it would require something of similar magnitude to move back to reading if that’s something that we decide is worth it and something we’re not willing to give up. And here I think it’s useful to return to how I opened the essay, which is with this metaphor about the Library of Alexandria. I wrote about that because the Library of Alexandria is known as this real intellectual center of this earlier era. And for a long time, people thought that the library was destroyed by an act of war, that Julius Caesar burned it. What the new consensus is among historians is that: No, these external attacks didn’t matter that much. The library died because people didn’t care that it lived. They neglected it, and then it eventually fell into disrepair. And so I think that is a really useful way to think about the present, that we just have to decide because we’re doing this to ourselves, that we want to choose differently. Rosin: Did doing this research change your own reading habits at all? [ Music ] Horowitch: I definitely have tried to read more. And I think what I find is just when I do have time, I always love reading and get back into it. But there’s always that little first hump that I have to get over, to kind of retrain my brain. I don’t know if that’s actually what’s happening inside my brain, but it feels like I can’t just get through it, and then I start to get engrossed, and then I stop looking at my phone as much. And so it’s definitely made me think about that much more and notice it more clearly. Rosin: I feel like that’s a no. ( Laughs .) Horowitch: ( Laughs .) I’m trying to be positive. I’m like, No, I should read more . Rosin: Shouldn’t we all. Well, Rose, thank you so much for joining me today. Horowitch: Thank you for having me. Rosin: This episode of Radio Atlantic was produced by Jinae West. It was edited by Kevin Townsend. Yvonne Kim fact-checked. Rob Smierciak engineered and provided original music. We also had music from Breakmaster Cylinder. Claudine Ebeid is the executive producer of Atlantic audio, and Andrea Valdez is our managing editor. Listeners, if you enjoy the show, you can support our work and the work of all Atlantic journalists when you subscribe to The Atlantic at TheAtlantic.com/Listener . I’m Hanna Rosin. Thank you for listening.

items.title: Plus de 140 migrants introuvables après le sauvetage d’une embarcation
items.description: Le gouvernement gambien a fait part de sa vive inquiétude après le sauvetage de 38 migrants au large de Nouadhibou, en Mauritanie. Alors qu'environ 180 personnes avaient pris place à bord de l’embarcation partie de Gambie, plus de 140 passagers demeurent portés disparus, ravivant les craintes autour de la dangereuse route migratoire de l'Atlantique. Le gouvernement de la Gambie a exprimé, mercredi 22 juillet 2026, sa profonde préoccupation à la suite du sauvetage d'une embarcation de migrants au large de Nouadhibou, dans le nord-ouest de la Mauritanie. Selon les premières informations communiquées par les autorités et relayées par des médias, le bateau avait quitté les côtes gambiennes avec environ 180 personnes à son bord, mais seuls 38 survivants ont été retrouvés à ce stade. D'après un communiqué du ministère gambien des Affaires étrangères, les rescapés ont été secourus le 18 juillet grâce à une opération menée par les garde-côtes mauritaniens, avec l'appui de pêcheurs artisanaux. Parmi eux figurent 34 hommes, deux femmes, une fille et un garçon. Les survivants ont raconté avoir dérivé pendant près de 25 jours en mer avant d'être retrouvés. Ils ont également indiqué que les passagers provenaient de plusieurs pays d'Afrique de l'Ouest, notamment de la Gambie, du Sénégal, de la Côte d'Ivoire, de la Guinée et du Mali. Les autorités mauritaniennes, en collaboration avec leurs homologues gambiennes et l'Organisation internationale pour les migrations (OIM), poursuivent les investigations afin d'identifier les survivants, de confirmer leur nationalité et de déterminer le sort des 140 personnes toujours portées disparues. Face à ce drame, le gouvernement gambien a adressé ses condoléances aux familles endeuillées et exprimé sa solidarité envers les pays dont des ressortissants pourraient compter parmi les victimes. Les autorités se sont également dites particulièrement bouleversées par le sort de deux enfants rescapés qui auraient perdu plusieurs membres de leur famille durant la traversée. À travers son ambassade à Nouakchott, Banjul affirme travailler étroitement avec les autorités mauritaniennes, l'OIM et les partenaires concernés, afin d'établir les circonstances exactes du naufrage, d'apporter une assistance consulaire aux ressortissants gambiens et de faire toute la lumière sur le destin des personnes disparues. Le gouvernement a, par ailleurs, réaffirmé son engagement à protéger ses citoyens et à renforcer la coopération régionale pour favoriser des migrations régulières et sûres, tout en intensifiant la lutte contre les réseaux de trafic de migrants et les facteurs qui alimentent les départs clandestins. Les autorités gambiennes ont également lancé un appel à leurs ressortissants, les invitant à privilégier les voies légales de migration et à éviter les traversées irrégulières, souvent synonymes de tragédies humaines. Ce nouveau drame rappelle une fois de plus la dangerosité de la route atlantique vers les îles Canaries, considérée comme l'une des plus meurtrières au monde. Selon l'OIM, au moins 1 214 migrants y ont perdu la vie ou disparu lors de 54 naufrages recensés en 2025. Quelques jours auparavant, le HCR signalait déjà la mort ou la disparition de 144 migrants au large de la Mauritanie entre le 14 et le 18 juillet, malgré le sauvetage de 387 personnes. Par Valentin SOMANDE L’article Plus de 140 migrants introuvables après le sauvetage d'une embarcation est apparu en premier sur Wakat Séra .

items.title: Marineland : huit dauphins transférés en Espagne
items.description: Des dauphins sautent lors d’un spectacle au parc à thème Marineland à Antibes, sur la Côte d’Azur (sud-est de la France), le 17 mars 2016. Les orques et les dauphins du Marineland d’Antibes (Alpes-Maritimes) ne peuvent pas être transférés en Espagne, suite à un avis défavorable de l’autorité scientifique espagnole, et doivent y rester pour le moment, a indiqué le cabinet du ministre français de la Transition écologique à l’AFP le 10 avril 2025. (Photo de VALERY HACHE / AFP) VALERY HACHE / AFP

items.title: Trump's nuclear deal with Saudis jettisons longstanding US demands
items.description: Signing a nuclear pact in the Middle East is a bold move that has drawn criticism in the US and in Israel.

items.title: La délocalisation du marché transfrontalier d'Ango-Ango/Noki suscite l'inquiétude des commerçants à Matadi
items.description: À Matadi, l'annonce du déplacement du marché transfrontalier d'Ango-Ango/Noki provoque une vive inquiétude chez les petits commerçants transfrontaliers. Ces derniers craignent une baisse d'activité et des risques sécuritaires.

items.title: Retrait du Burkina Faso, du Mali et du Niger de la CPI : fin de partie de la justice pénale internationale ?
items.description: Le Burkina Faso, le Mali et le Niger, réunis au sein de l'Alliance des États du Sahel (AES), ont annoncé, le 30 juin 2026, leur retrait de la Cour pénale internationale (CPI). Cette décision s'inscrit dans une stratégie plus large de distanciation vis-à-vis de plusieurs organisations régionales et internationales. Après leur départ de la Communauté économique des États de l'Afrique de l'Ouest (CEDEAO), effectif en janvier 2025, puis celui de l'Organisation internationale de la Francophonie (OIF), en mars 2025, les trois États poursuivent dans la voie de la redéfinition de leurs relations avec des institutions qu'ils jugent inféodées à la France et, plus largement, aux puissances occidentales. Pour autant, il serait excessif d'y voir une volonté d'isolement. Le Burkina Faso, le Mali et le Niger demeurent membres de plusieurs organisations africaines, comme la Communauté des États sahélo-sahariens (CEN-SAD) [1] , ainsi que d'organisations à vocation continentale comme la Zone de libre-échange continentale africaine (ZLECAf). Entretien avec Caroline Roussy, directrice de recherche à l’IRIS, reponsable du programme Afrique/s. Dans quel contexte s'inscrit le retrait de la CPI ? Le 22 septembre 2025, les États membres de l'AES, que sont le Burkina Faso, le Mali et le Niger, ont annoncé leur intention de se retirer « immédiatement » de la Cour pénale internationale (CPI), dénonçant un « instrument de répression néocoloniale aux mains de l'impérialisme ». Comprendre, en intertexte à peine voilé, une dénonciation de ce qu'ils présentent comme l'impérialisme tutélaire de la France et, plus largement, de l'Occident. Toutefois, cette annonce n'avait, à l'époque, pas été suivie d'effets, puisqu'aucune notification n'avait alors été adressée au Secrétaire général des Nations unies. Cette procédure n'a finalement été engagée que le 30 juin 2026. Comment expliquer ce laps de temps de près de neuf mois ? En l'état, seules quelques hypothèses peuvent être avancées. Les difficultés rencontrées par les autorités maliennes face au blocus imposé par le JNIM [2] à l'automne 2025, puis aux offensives coordonnées du JNIM et du Front de Libération de l’Azawad (FLA), ont-elles pesé dans le calendrier ? Les régimes militaires craignent-ils également que certaines exactions documentées au Sahel central puissent, à terme, attirer l'attention de la CPI ? Aucune explication officielle n'a été avancée. Peut-être aussi que, face à la hiérarchie des urgences, ce projet a simplement été reporté. Dans tous les cas, les chefs d'État s'y étaient projetés depuis plusieurs mois. Quoi qu'il en soit, António Guterres, secrétaire général des Nations unies, a enregistré la notification officielle qui lui a été transmise. Le retrait ne deviendra effectif que dans un an. En effet, en vertu de l'article 127 du Statut de Rome, un État doit notifier officiellement sa décision et le retrait ne prend effet qu'un an après cette notification. « Affirmer pleinement leur souveraineté », tel est le message politique que les trois chefs d'État souhaitent envoyer. Cependant, par-delà les éléments de langage de communication politique, force est de constater que cette souveraineté, du moins sur le plan sécuritaire, demeure largement tributaire du soutien russe, auquel s'ajoute, au Niger, une coopération croissante avec la Turquie. Dans leur déclaration commune de septembre 2025, les dirigeants de l'AES estimaient que la CPI « s'[était] montrée incapable de prendre en charge et de juger les crimes de guerre, les crimes contre l'humanité, les crimes de génocide et les crimes d'agression avérés ». Clairement, ils dénonçaient une politique du « deux poids, deux mesures ». Dans le contexte de la guerre menée par Israël à Gaza, qualifiée de génocide par plusieurs États et organisations internationales, la CPI avait délivré, le 21 novembre 2024, un mandat d'arrêt contre le Premier ministre israélien Benyamin Netanyahou. Pourtant, son exécution demeure, jusqu'à aujourd'hui, lettre morte. Et il convient de le souligner, certains États, dont la France, considèrent qu'Israël, n'étant pas partie au Statut de Rome – texte fondateur de la CPI –, son Premier ministre bénéficie des immunités reconnues par le droit international. Le ministère français des Affaires étrangères a ainsi déclaré dans un communiqué qu'« on ne peut exiger d'un État qu'il agisse en contradiction avec les obligations qui lui incombent en vertu du droit international en ce qui concerne les immunités des États qui ne sont pas parties à la CPI ». De quoi faire grincer des dents et nourrir les rancœurs… Par ailleurs, la critique de la CPI n'est pas nouvelle sur le continent africain. Depuis de nombreuses années, plusieurs chefs d'État, tout autant que l'Union africaine (UA), dénoncent une institution qui a principalement poursuivi des responsables africains, comme en témoigne l'affaire de Laurent Gbagbo. Mais considérer que seuls les Africains font l’objet de poursuites internationales, mérite d'être nuancée. Interrogé par RFI, Julien Antouly, maître de conférences en droit international, rappelle que, si la CPI a longtemps été critiquée pour ses enquêtes focalisées sur l'Afrique, elle a depuis élargi son périmètre d'action. Plus de la moitié des situations actuellement examinées concernent désormais des États situés en dehors du continent africain, notamment l'Ukraine, la Palestine, le Bangladesh/Myanmar, le Venezuela ou encore les Philippines. Antouly s'étonne également des accusations formulées par l’AES d’autant que « dans le cas précis des États de l'AES, ces accusations [de partialité] [lui] semblent un tout petit peu abusives puisque la CPI n'a pas mené d'action extrêmement hostile à l'encontre de ces trois États ». Ce retrait fragilise-t-il la justice pénale internationale ? Pas tout à fait. D'abord parce que la CPI ne suscite pas l'adhésion de l'ensemble des États. Plusieurs grandes puissances, parmi lesquelles les États-Unis, la Chine et la Russie – soit trois des cinq membres permanents du Conseil de sécurité de l’ONU –, mais aussi Israël, ne sont pas parties au Statut de Rome. Depuis son origine, la CPI fonctionne sans la participation de ces États. Par ailleurs, il convient d'être précis : le retrait des trois États de l'AES n'aura aucune incidence sur les enquêtes et procédures déjà engagées concernant des crimes commis avant la date effective de leur retrait. La Cour conservera sa compétence pour ces faits. En revanche, cette décision témoigne d'une défiance croissante à l'égard du système international et, plus particulièrement, de la justice pénale internationale, perçue comme appliquant le droit de manière sélective et comme étant incapable de garantir l'exécution effective de ses propres décisions. Si d'autres États venaient à suivre cette voie, la capacité de la CPI à exercer ses mandats, tout autant que sa légitimité, pourraient être écornées. Cependant, malgré les critiques récurrentes dont la CPI fait l'objet et les menaces de retrait régulièrement brandies par certains États, Julien Antouly souligne que le nombre d'États parties au Statut de Rome est demeuré relativement stable au fil des années. Le retrait des trois États de l'AES constitue, certes, un signal politique fort mais il ne remet ni en cause l'existence ni le fonctionnement de la justice pénale internationale. Quelles conséquences concrètes ce retrait aura-t-il ? À court terme, les conséquences juridiques de ce retrait demeurent limitées puisque, comme indiqué précédemment, son entrée en vigueur n'interviendra qu'un an après sa notification. D'ici là, les trois États restent tenus de coopérer avec la Cour et celle-ci conserve sa compétence pour les crimes commis avant la date effective de leur retrait. Le cas du Mali est d'ailleurs particulier puisqu'une enquête est déjà ouverte depuis 2012, à la suite d'un renvoi effectué par les autorités maliennes elles-mêmes et ce après que des crimes ont été commis dans le contexte du conflit armé dans le nord du Mali. En revanche, le principal enjeu dans les mois à venir sera d'ordre opérationnel : si les États concernés, et en particulier le Mali, décident de ne plus coopérer avec la Cour, sa capacité à poursuivre ses enquêtes et à recueillir des preuves pourrait s'en retrouver fortement altérée. Les conséquences de ce retrait pour les populations victimes de la déflagration sécuritaire font toutefois l'objet d'appréciations divergentes. Pour Julien Antouly, la fermeture de cette voie de recours ne signifie pas que toute perspective de justice disparaît. La CPI n'intervient, selon lui, qu'en complément des juridictions nationales et d'autres mécanismes régionaux, comme la Cour africaine des droits de l'homme et des peuples, qui peuvent également être mobilisés. La justice pénale internationale constitue donc une voie parmi d'autres mais n'a jamais été la plus efficace pour les victimes au Sahel [3] . Cette analyse est toutefois loin d'être partagée par plusieurs organisations de défense des droits humains. La Fédération internationale pour les droits humains (FIDH) estime au contraire que « la décision de se retirer de la CPI fragilise la situation des victimes, pour lesquelles la Cour représente souvent le dernier espoir d’obtenir justice. Après leur retrait de la CEDEAO, la perte de la protection de la CPI laisse les victimes au Burkina Faso, au Mali et au Niger sans recours pour les violations des droits humains les plus graves qu’elles continuent de subir », comme l’a déclaré Drissa Traoré, secrétaire général de la FIDH. « Dans ces pays qui subissent une crise multidimensionnelle, les juridictions nationales ne sont toujours pas en mesure de fournir justice et réparations aux victimes, en raison d’un manque de volonté politique et d’une incapacité à enquêter sur les crimes de guerre et les crimes contre l’humanité ». Au-delà de ce débat juridique, une douloureuse réalité demeure. Après près d'une décennie de conflits, le Burkina Faso, le Mali et le Niger comptent plusieurs millions de personnes déplacées internes et des milliers de victimes des attaques perpétrées par les groupes djihadistes, mais aussi d'exactions attribuées à certaines forces de défense et de sécurité ainsi qu'aux supplétifs russes de Wagner puis d' Africa Corps . Dans ce contexte, les questions essentielles qui se posent sont les suivantes : les populations auront-elles, demain, effectivement accès à la justice, qu'elle soit nationale, régionale ou internationale ? Pourront-elles faire prévaloir leurs droits ? Pourront-elles être reconnues dans leur statut de victimes ? Au-delà de la dignité retrouvée, c'est une question de droit, mais aussi, peut-être, de cohésion nationale à l’échelle de chacun des pays concernés. [1] Les pays membres de la CEN-SAD sont les suivants : Bénin, Burkina Faso, République centrafricaine, Comores, Côte d'Ivoire, Djibouti, Égypte, Érythrée, Gambie, Ghana, Guinée, Guinée-Bissau, Libye, Mali, Maroc, Mauritanie, Niger, Nigeria, Sénégal, Sierra Leone, Somalie, Soudan, Tchad, Togo et Tunisie. [2] L’acronyme JNIM en langue française est le GSIM soit le Groupe de soutien à l’islam et aux musulmans. [3] Voir op.cit. L’article Retrait du Burkina Faso, du Mali et du Niger de la CPI : fin de partie de la justice pénale internationale ? est apparu en premier sur IRIS .

items.title: When Egypt tried to build its own missile program
items.description: What happens when a postcolonial state attempts to challenge the global concentration of advanced technology? From artificial intelligence to nuclear technology and advanced weapons, this question has once again moved to the center of global politics. Yet the struggle over who possesses advanced knowledge, who is permitted to develop it and who claims the authority to deny it to others has a much longer history. On July 23, 1962, exactly 10 years after Egypt’s revolution, former President Gamal Abdel Nasser offered his own answer. Rockets rose over the Egyptian desert as Egypt marked the anniversary of the 1952 revolution. Nasser declared that the country had entered the missile age. The spectacle reflected ambitions that extended well beyond the missiles themselves. Emerging from colonial rule and repeated military defeats, Egypt sought to demonstrate that it could acquire and produce advanced technologies long-monopolized by the industrialized world. For Nasser, the missile program was not simply about military power. It formed part of a broader project of technological sovereignty: the belief that political independence remained incomplete without the capacity to master modern science and industry. The response quickly became international. Israeli intelligence launched a covert campaign against the German scientists helping build the program through intimidation, kidnappings, letter bombs and assassination attempts. West Germany struggled to balance its growing economic and diplomatic ties with the Arab world against mounting Israeli pressure and the moral and political legacies of the Second World War. Washington increasingly viewed Egypt’s ambitions through the language of regional stability, proliferation and its evolving security partnership with Israel. Even the Soviet Union, Cairo’s principal military patron, remained reluctant to see Egypt acquire capabilities beyond Moscow’s control. At the center of these competing interests stood a group of German scientists and engineers whose expertise transformed a national development project into an international political crisis. More than 60 years later, the questions raised by Egypt’s missile program remain remarkably familiar. Who gets to master the technologies of the future? And who claims the authority to decide? A postcolonial quest for technological sovereignty Egypt’s missile ambitions grew out of military defeat. The Nakba of 1948 exposed the weaknesses of Arab armies, while the 1956 Suez Crisis reinforced Egypt’s dependence on technologically superior powers. Although Nasser emerged politically strengthened from Suez, the conflict underscored that military independence remained constrained by technological dependence. His ambitions, therefore, extended beyond acquiring better weapons. Across the postcolonial world, newly independent states invested in dams, factories, universities, laboratories and industry, not only to accelerate development, but also to reduce dependence on former colonial powers. Egypt’s missile program belonged to this broader effort. Developing an indigenous missile capability promised military advantages, but it also symbolized the belief that advanced scientific knowledge need not remain the monopoly of the industrialized world. Yet ambition alone could not overcome structural constraints. Egypt lacked the industrial base, the scientific infrastructure and the specialized personnel needed to build advanced missile systems. Purchasing weapons abroad offered one solution, but dependence on foreign suppliers inevitably limited Egyptian autonomy. Even the Soviet Union, despite its close partnership with Cairo, remained unwilling to transfer every technology Egypt sought. Great powers, regardless of ideology, were reluctant to relinquish control over the knowledge underpinning strategic superiority. Egyptian policymakers, therefore, pursued a different strategy. Rather than importing weapons alone, they sought to import expertise by recruiting foreign scientists and engineers capable of establishing an indigenous missile industry. West Germany became a particularly attractive source of that expertise. Scientists and engineers with wartime and postwar experience in rocketry and aerospace found limited opportunities to pursue ambitious projects at home, while Egypt offered them something increasingly scarce in postwar Europe: the opportunity to build. Dozens eventually accepted contracts connected to Egyptian aviation and missile projects, transforming an ambitious national development project into an international geopolitical crisis. The German scientists who became a crisis For Israel, the presence of German scientists in Egypt carried historical and strategic significance that was impossible to separate. German experts were helping an Arab government develop missiles that Israeli leaders feared could eventually threaten the country’s cities. Whatever the technical limitations of Egypt’s program in the early 1960s, its political and psychological significance was profound. The controversy, however, was never simply about missiles. It was about who was permitted to acquire advanced technological capabilities. Egypt viewed foreign expertise as essential to reducing its technological dependence and strengthening its sovereignty. Israel, by contrast, regarded an indigenous Egyptian missile industry as an unacceptable strategic challenge. The struggle was therefore as much about preserving a regional technological advantage as it was about countering an immediate military threat. Israeli intelligence closely monitored the German scientists and the network of European front companies, suppliers and intermediaries that procured equipment and materials for the Egyptian missile program, before launching a covert campaign to disrupt it. As part of Israel’s Operation Damocles, letter bombs wounded staff at the Egyptian missile program’s procurement offices in Europe. Likewise, rocket specialist Heinz Krug disappeared in Munich in 1962, widely believed to have been abducted and killed by the Mossad. Rather than targeting missiles alone, Israeli operations targeted the people who possessed the expertise Egypt sought to acquire. The campaign demonstrated a broader reality that continues to shape international politics: restricting the movement of knowledge can prove as strategically important as restricting the movement of weapons. The episode also unfolded within a broader history of Arab-German relations that is often overlooked when reduced to another chapter of the Arab-Israeli conflict. For many Arab governments, West Germany had become an increasingly attractive economic and technological partner. It possessed advanced industrial expertise and, unlike Britain and France, was not directly associated with the region’s recent colonial rule. For Bonn, the Arab world offered expanding markets and another arena in its Cold War rivalry with East Germany. Egypt’s recruitment of German scientists emerged from these wider political, economic and scientific relationships. The missile program, nevertheless, became a diplomatic crisis. Bonn sought to preserve its expanding economic and diplomatic ties with Arab governments, while responding to mounting Israeli pressure rooted in West Germany’s postwar commitment to reconciliation with Israel and its broader reckoning with the Holocaust. Yet it possessed only limited legal authority to prevent private citizens from accepting employment abroad. Washington, meanwhile, viewed Egypt’s ambitions through the language of regional stability, proliferation and alliance management, reflecting not only genuine security concerns, but also a preference for preserving a regional military balance favorable to Israel. The episode exposed a dilemma that remains strikingly contemporary. States can restrict the sale of equipment, impose export controls and disrupt procurement networks. Controlling expertise is far more difficult. Scientists cross borders. Knowledge circulates. Technical experience cannot be embargoed as easily as hardware. Long before governments competed over artificial intelligence, semiconductors or quantum computing, they were already competing over the people capable of producing the technologies that mattered most. Why the missiles matter less than we think Egypt’s native missile program ultimately fell short of Nasser’s ambitions. Technical obstacles persisted, particularly in guidance and control systems. The program demanded financial, industrial and human resources that Egypt struggled to sustain. Israeli covert operations, together with West German pressure that encouraged scientists to return home, further weakened the project. Egypt’s defeat in the Naksa of 1967 ultimately brought the program to an end. It is tempting to judge the project solely by the missiles it failed to produce. Doing so misses its broader significance. The international response was shaped less by what Egypt had achieved than by what it might eventually become capable of achieving. The controversy revolved around future technological possibilities, rather than existing military realities. An engineer could cross a border without carrying a single missile component and still transfer decades of accumulated knowledge in aerodynamics, guidance systems, manufacturing and program management. Expertise itself had become a strategic resource. The struggle therefore extended beyond rockets and production facilities to scientists, laboratories and the circulation of knowledge across borders. Controlling expertise became another means of determining which states could realistically aspire to technological autonomy and which would remain dependent on external suppliers. Seen in this way, Egypt was pursuing what we would now call technological sovereignty: the capacity to develop and master critical technologies without remaining permanently dependent on more powerful states. The missile program ultimately failed to achieve that objective. Yet the extraordinary international effort devoted to undermining it revealed something larger than the fate of a single weapons program. The concern was not only the missiles that Egypt might build, but the possibility that a postcolonial state could acquire scientific and industrial capabilities that reduced its long-term dependence on external powers. Such capabilities promised not only military strength, but also greater political and strategic autonomy, challenging an international order in which advanced technological knowledge remained concentrated among a small number of states. The technology wars are not new The technologies at the center of geopolitical competition have changed dramatically since Abdel Nasser’s missiles first appeared over the Egyptian desert. The politics surrounding them are remarkably familiar. Today, governments compete over artificial intelligence, nuclear technology and advanced weapons. They impose export controls, restrict technology transfers, scrutinize scientific collaborations, sanction firms and compete to attract or deny highly skilled scientists and engineers. The struggle increasingly revolves around whether access to expertise today might allow a rival to develop a capability tomorrow. The history of Egypt’s missile program places these contemporary contexts within a much longer trajectory of global technological competition. It also exposes the unequal hierarchies that continue to shape the international system. Technological ambitions are rarely judged according to technical capability alone. They are filtered through political relationships and international hierarchies in which the identity of the state pursuing them often determines whether technological development is celebrated as innovation, accepted as modernization or condemned as proliferation. This does not mean that every state’s technological ambitions are equally benign, nor that security concerns are merely rhetorical. It does suggest, however, that similar technological pursuits are rarely evaluated according to consistent standards. States closely aligned with the prevailing international order have often enjoyed greater latitude in developing advanced military technologies than postcolonial states seeking to reduce their technological dependence. The language of security has often functioned not only to address genuine threats, but also to regulate who is permitted to acquire the capabilities that define power in a given era. Nasser’s missiles never transformed Egypt into the technological power he envisioned. Yet the crisis surrounding the German scientists revealed something more enduring than the fate of a single missile program. It exposed how expertise itself became an object of geopolitical competition and how powerful states sought to regulate not only the movement of weapons, but also the movement of knowledge. More than 60 years later, the question that confronted Cairo, Bonn, Tel Aviv, Washington and Moscow remains unresolved: Who gets to master the technologies of the future? More importantly, who claims the authority to decide? Acknowledgments: This essay is part of a broader research project on the missile age and proliferation in the Arab world during the Cold War, with a particular focus on Egypt and Arab-German relations. The author gratefully acknowledges the support of the Arab-German Young Academy of Sciences and Humanities (AGYA) through its Research Mobility Program, which supported the research and writing of this essay. The post When Egypt tried to build its own missile program first appeared on Mada Masr .

items.title: US Officials Sound Alarm on Haiti While Preparing to Deport Haitians There
items.description: Click to expand Image Members of the Haitian community rally in support for the extension of TPS and against deportation in Miami, Florida April 26, 2026. © 2026 Carl Juste/Miami Herald/TNS via Reuters Fresh from a trip to Haiti, US Ambassador to the United Nations Mike Waltz offered the UN Security Council on July 20 a harrowing account of the “kill zone” he said Port-au-Prince has become. In unusually graphic detail, he recounted grotesque incidents, including one in which several Haitian national police officers “were flayed alive in the streets [and] pinned to the side of their burning armored vehicle.” Consistent with Waltz’s report, a July 10 State Department travel advisory warns: “Do Not Travel to Haiti,” citing “robbery, carjackings, sexual assault, and kidnappings,” and even the “risk of being struck by stray bullets,” saying that violence “happens frequently.” And yet, the same government that describes horrors before the UN Security Council and certifies the country as too dangerous to travel to is preparing to deport some 350,000 Haitians there, as the US Supreme Court stands aside and allows the termination of Temporary Protected Status (TPS). Mass deportations would put these people in grave danger and exacerbate Haiti’s humanitarian crisis. Many would be compelled to rejoin families living in dangerous areas where, perceived as wealthy, they could become targets for kidnapping and extortion. It’s not too late. A new Homeland Security secretary is in place, Markwayne Mullin, who could still recognize Haiti as unsafe and extend TPS. It will take some convincing: after the Supreme Court ruling, he called upon Haitians to self-deport, offering them $2,100 to go back. But maybe the fact that the Federal Aviation Administration prohibits US air carrier flights to Port-au-Prince due to ongoing instability will give him pause. The Supreme Court’s ruling is due to go into effect later this month. Secretary Mullin should listen to the words of the US ambassador to the UN and heed the State Department’s warning that, in fact, applies as much to Haitians as to US citizens: Do Not Travel to Haiti.

items.title: Hong Kong’s competition watchdog tightens tender clauses to target bid-rigging
items.description: Hong Kong’s competition watchdog has revised its tender clauses to require bidders to make a statutory declaration that they have not engaged in collusion on a project, as part of a wider move to criminalise bid-rigging following the city’s deadliest fire in decades. Senior counsel Jat Sew-tong, chairman of the Competition Commission, also said on Thursday the body was considering proposing to raise the maximum penalty for the proposed criminal offence from seven to 10 years. Tendering practices...

items.description: MOSCOW, (Sputnik) - Global oil prices are rising, with the price of a barrel of Brent crude reaching above $99 for the first time since May22, trading data showed on Thursday.

items.title: What is Pickaxe Mountain, the Iranian nuclear-linked site threatened by Trump?
items.description: United States President Donald Trump has threatened to attack a site linked to Iran’s nuclear programme known as Pickaxe Mountain, a fortified facility buried deep underground near one of Tehran’s main nuclear sites . The threat reflects escalating tensions as Tehran and Washington trade fire in the ​Gulf, setting back efforts to end the conflict. Here is what we know about Pickaxe Mountain: What is Pickaxe Mountain? Pickaxe Mountain is located 220 kilometres (140 ‌miles) south of Tehran and 2km (1.2 miles) from the Natanz nuclear complex. The Natanz site, where two of Iran’s uranium enrichment plants were located, was bombed during the war started by the United States and Israel on February 28, and during last year’s 12-day war . The tunnel facility under construction at Pickaxe Mountain was not targeted in either of those wars, according to the Institute for Science ​and International Security (ISIS), a US-based think-tank focused on nuclear non-proliferation. The peak rises to some 1,600 metres above sea level. There were two enrichment plants in operation ​at Natanz — one above and one below ground. The UN nuclear watchdog has said the above-ground one was destroyed. The ⁠other, underground one was likely at least badly damaged. An Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesperson said on July 22 that there was no nuclear activity at Pickaxe Mountain. What is the history of the site? The site is linked to Iran’s nuclear programme, which has long caused tension between the West and Iran, which denies seeking an atomic bomb. Construction of the ​facility at Pickaxe Mountain began in 2020, according to ISIS, following what Iranian authorities reported at the time as an explosion caused by an act of sabotage at the Natanz facility. Iran said at the time the Natanz sabotage had caused significant damage that could slow the development of advanced uranium enrichment centrifuges. In September that year, Iran’s then-nuclear chief, Ali Akbar Salehi, said Iran had ​started building “a more modern, larger and more comprehensive hall in all dimensions in the heart of the mountain near Natanz” for making advanced centrifuges. Rafael Grossi, the chief of ​the UN nuclear watchdog, in an interview with PBS Frontline in March, noted that Iran had previously announced its intention to have nuclear activity at Pickaxe Mountain. It was part of what ‌he called ⁠a systematic Iranian intention to put its most sensitive facilities underground. What has Iran built at Pickaxe Mountain ISIS, which has analysed satellite imagery of the site, says it features two pairs of entrances, which are assumed to lead to one facility estimated to be at least 100 metres under the mountain. The physical defensive measures consist primarily of a large security perimeter and the extensive hardening of tunnel entrances, ISIS said in a July 14 report . The pair of eastern tunnel portal entrances have been partially backfilled since ​the wars to obstruct ground vehicle ​access, but they have not been ⁠fully sealed, the ISIS report said. Sam Lair, a fellow at the Foreign Policy Research Institute, who also reviewed recent satellite imagery of the site, told Reuters that increasing the strength of the tunnel entrances would complicate targeting it with penetrating munitions such ​as bunker busters. Is the state functioning and what could it be used for? Trump said this month that the US was ​watching the site closely ⁠and did not see any activity there. ISIS, in its report, said its assessment was that the facility was not yet operational, but that construction continued there and that it was unclear when it could be operational, based on satellite imagery alone. It said if Iran started to rebuild its centrifuge manufacturing capability, it could plan to install a smaller centrifuge ⁠assembly facility ​in Pickaxe Mountain able to serve a nuclear weapons programme. How might the site be attacked? Experts assess ​the deeply buried complex is beyond the reach of the most powerful bunker-buster bombs in the US arsenal. ISIS said the site would be more suitable for ground forces to attack or sabotage, but that ​vulnerabilities may also exist that could be exploited by deep earth-penetrating weapons via aerial attacks.
